items.md: DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald borrows Grandma Duck's colorblind old bull and his cart to enter a contest for the most old-fashioned rig at the Farmers' Fair. *(Jan. 6, 1955)*

items.md:
Front cover: The nephews route their model railroad around the sleeping Donald. *(June 23, 1955)*
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Gyro has invented a gun for Donald that shoots custard, temporarily blinding game and letting the hunter capture it alive. *(Mar. 17, 1955)*
Gyro, although mentioned, does not appear in the story.
Reprinted: Walt Disney's Comics No. 399, December 1973.

items.md: DONALD DUCK - 10 - The nephews vow on Christmas Eve never to take another bath, and flee when Donald summons them to the tub. *(Mar. 31, 1955)*
items.value: DONALD DUCK - 10 - The nephews vow on Christmas Eve never to take another bath, and flee when Donald summons them to the tub. (Mar. 31, 1955)

items.md: # 185 (16/5) - February 1956 - 36 pages
items.value: 185 (16/5) - February 1956 - 36 pages

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - At New Year's, Donald resolves to serve only the nephews' favorite foods, while the nephews resolve to eat only the foods that Donald has said are good for them. *(Apr. 21, 1955)*
Cross-references: The ducks refer clearly to the events of "DONALD DUCK" in Walt Disney's Comics No. 173, February 1955.
Reprinted: Walt Disney's Comics No. 377, February 1972.
items.value:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - At New Year's, Donald resolves to serve only the nephews' favorite foods, while the nephews resolve to eat only the foods that Donald has said are good for them. (Apr. 21, 1955)
Cross-references: The ducks refer clearly to the events of "DONALD DUCK" in Walt Disney's Comics No. 173, February 1955.
Reprinted: Walt Disney's Comics No. 377, February 1972.

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald and the nephews have rival ice taxis to carry fishermen to fishing holes across a frozen lake. *(Apr. 21, 1955)*
A new version of "This Month's Thriller - Rival Boatmen" in Walt Disney's Comics No. 45, June 1944. (Barks pointed out the resemblance in a June 1956 letter to one of his editors at Western Publishing.)
Reprinted: Walt Disney Comics Digest No. 33, February 1972.
items.value:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald and the nephews have rival ice taxis to carry fishermen to fishing holes across a frozen lake. (Apr. 21, 1955)
A new version of "This Month's Thriller - Rival Boatmen" in Walt Disney's Comics No. 45, June 1944. (Barks pointed out the resemblance in a June 1956 letter to one of his editors at Western Publishing.)
Reprinted: Walt Disney Comics Digest No. 33, February 1972.

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Scrooge gives Donald a featherbed business and Gladstone a moving business to see which is best qualified to manage his fortune. *(Apr. 28, 1955)*
Reprinted: Walt Disney's Comics No. 385, October 1972.
items.value:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Scrooge gives Donald a featherbed business and Gladstone a moving business to see which is best qualified to manage his fortune. (Apr. 28, 1955)
Reprinted: Walt Disney's Comics No. 385, October 1972.

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald enters the Duckburg tryouts for the Olympic team, but finishes in only one event, and with no one but the nephews there to see him. *(Apr. 28, 1955)*
Barks caricatured himself on the sixth page of this story as Fulldrip Pulpbugle, a javelin hurler with hay fever.
items.value:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald enters the Duckburg tryouts for the Olympic team, but finishes in only one event, and with no one but the nephews there to see him. (Apr. 28, 1955)
Barks caricatured himself on the sixth page of this story as Fulldrip Pulpbugle, a javelin hurler with hay fever.

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - When gophers, cut-worms and birds prevent Donald from growing a shade tree in his back
yard, he buys the world's toughest tree — the Mongolian Thorned Mulberry. *(June 30, 1955)*
items.value:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - When gophers, cut-worms and birds prevent Donald from growing a shade tree in his back
yard, he buys the world's toughest tree — the Mongolian Thorned Mulberry. (June 30, 1955)

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald and the nephews hold a swimming race to decide if the nephews will take their piano lessons without complaining. *(July 14, 1955)*
A new version of "DONALD DUCK" in Walt Disney's Comics No. 54, March 1945. (Barks pointed out the resemblance in a June 1956 letter to one of his editors at Western Publishing.)

items.md:
DONALD DUCK - 10 - Donald uses a scintillator and uranium buttons on their caps to keep track of the nephews when the ducks camp in the woods. *(Sept. 1, 1955)*
Reprinted: Walt Disney's Comics No. 406, July 1974.

Reprinted: Donald Duck No. 159, October 1974.
